content: A B S T R A C T Understanding, modeling, and predicting transfer in membrane processes remain major challenges, as they involve several coupled phenomena occurring in concentrated, non-ideal phases. Current models often treat hydrodynamic, colloidal, and interfacial effects separately, making it difficult to link microscopic in­teractions to macroscopic behavior such as permeation, selectivity, and fouling. This work proposes an interaction-based framework that describes membrane transport through the fundamental couplings between the fluid, colloid, and membrane phases. By reformulating transport equations in terms of these interactions, the approach reveals the minimal set of ingredients and interactions required to capture the essence of membrane transfer phenomena — the ultimate reduction before losing physical meaning. The resulting framework provides a continuous description from dilute dispersions to concentrated or consolidated states, bridging thermodynamic and me­chanical regimes. Finally, the framework is applied to three representative cases illustrating how interaction complexity shapes separation. Beyond offering a new way to model transfer, this perspective helps recover the physical sense of membrane separation: connecting intuition, equations, and mechanisms into a coherent view of how membranes actually operate.

content: 1. Introduction Understanding transfer in membrane processes remains a central challenge in separation science. Mass transport through a membrane involves multiple coupled phenomena — hydrodynamic flow, diffusion, colloidal, and interfacial interactions — occurring in concentrated, nonideal environments. Despite decades of research, modeling and pre­dicting these coupled mechanisms remain difficult, as they bridge vastly different scales in space, time, and physical nature. At the core of membrane processes lie interactions between three fundamental components: the fluid, the colloids -we will use the term colloids here to refer to all types of Brownian objects (solute, macro­molecules, particles) exerting osmotic pressure-, and the membrane. These interactions in aqueous dispersion may arise from electrostatic, electro-steric, van der Waals, or hydration forces, each acting over characteristic length scales ranging from several hundred nanometers to a few nanometers. Their importance stems from a geometrical coinci­dence: the characteristic size of membrane pores is often comparable to that of the species being separated. Consequently, these interaction forces play a dominant role in shaping transport, accumulation, and selectivity within confined geometries. The significance of such interactions was already underlined in the

citation.title: Membrane transport explained with a colloidal interaction-based framework

content:
1990s by Cohen and Probstein [1], who formulated the flux paradox in colloidal filtration: the experimentally observed permeate flux could not be explained solely by hydrodynamics and convection–diffusion coupling. This paradox led to the definition of the critical flux [2,3], corresponding to the convective intensity required to overcome surface repulsion and initiate particle deposition. Since then, membrane trans­port phenomena have been described through distinct theoretical frameworks — mechanical, hydrodynamic, or thermodynamic — each focusing on specific regimes such as concentration polarization or cake/gel formation. However, these compartmentalized descriptions often lack a unified physical representation linking microscopic in­teractions to macroscopic transfer and selectivity. A continuous under­standing of interaction-driven states, from dilute dispersions to condensed and solid phases, is essential to predict and control process performance. Building on this historical foundation, the present paper presents an approach that revisits the importance of interactions in membrane transfer such as those encountered in Reverse-Osmosis, Nanofiltration, Ultrafiltration or in Forward Osmosis and Pressure Retarded Osmosis. Rather than starting from equations, it begins with physical intuition: identifying how the couplings induced by colloid–colloid and col­loid–membrane interactions govern transport. From this intuitive basis,

The approach proposed here is deliberately reductionist and peda­gogical. It starts from a simple triptych — fluid, colloid, and membrane — the essential ingredients of a good separation recipe. Within this framework, five pairwise fundamental interactions emerge: fluid–fluid, colloid–colloid, colloid–fluid, fluid–membrane, and colloid–membrane (Section 2). Yet, as in gastronomy, having the right ingredients is not enough: what truly defines the success of the recipe is how these in­gredients combine and interact. An interaction-centered formulation is then developed to reformulate the transport equations in terms of ex­change terms between the three ingredients (Section 3). The “cooking” begins where these interactions shape the behavior of the ingredients, giving rise to the physical mechanisms of permeation, polarization, partition, and osmosis (Section 4). The final section explores how the non-idealities of the ingredients enrich the recipe (Section 5), enabling continuous modeling from dilute to solid phases and opening perspec­tives for the design of advanced membrane materials with threedimensional control of interactions (Section 6). 2.

Membrane processes: a matter of interactions in a three ingredients recipe The mechanisms controlling transfer in membrane processes are inherently complex, as they involve several coupled phenomena occurring within highly non-ideal, often concentrated, phases. To distill this complexity down to its essential ingredients, three phases must be considered. - F: the fluid that flows through the membrane (e.g., water),- C: the colloid dispersed in the fluid (a generic term used to represent solutes or particles with surface interactions),- M: the membrane, which interacts specifically with both the fluid and the solute. These three ingredients form the base of the “recipe” for any mem­brane process. The richness — and the beauty — of membrane science come from the intricate entanglement of multiple interactions among these three phases. To embrace this complexity with enthusiasm rather than fear, we might recall the words of the French geneticist Albert Jacquard [4]:“Interactions induce an increase of complexity that can be the source of unexpected performances.” This statement resonates deeply in membrane science: interactions are the true source of the “magic” behind the surprising behaviors of membranes, whether biological or artificial.

Mastering membrane pro­cesses means understanding the interplay between these interactions and the coupled phenomena of hydrodynamics, diffusion, and interfa­cial forces. For many membranologists, the Graal is to harness this complexity — not to simplify it away — to unlock new opportunities in separation. 2.1. Inter-phase interactions: where the recipe begins A convenient way to visualize these interactions is through a ternary diagram (Fig. 1), whose vertices represent the three fundamental phases: Membrane (M), Fluid (F), and Colloid (C). This representation, also known to describe membrane formation by non-solvent phase inversion, provides an intuitive framework to grasp how the different interactions control mass transfer. Three major inter-phase interactions shape the behavior of membrane processes. - FM: Fluid–Membrane interactions Describe the friction of the fluid along the membrane thickness (active and support layers) and are expressed macroscopically through the membrane permeability. - CF: Colloid–Fluid interactions Govern the relative motion between colloids and the surrounding fluid -this relative motion is the basis of separation-, thus defining the transport properties of the suspension.

Intra-phase interactions: the internal quality of the ingredients Beyond the interfaces, interactions also occur within each phase. These intra-phase interactions reflect what could be seen as the internal quality of the ingredients themselves — how cohesive, structured, or responsive each component is under stress or concentration. - FF: Fluid–Fluid molecules interactions Represented by the fluid pressure; in a FF gradient, they define the driving force for the convective transport of the solvent. - CfC: Colloid–Colloid interactions (mediated by the fluid) Give rise to osmotic pressure, which governs the diffusive flux of colloids and represents the collective effects due to the concentration. 2.3. Optional direct interactions: when direct contact is made Under some conditions, direct mechanical (m) contact between phases or particles adds another layer of complexity. - MmC: direct Colloid–Membrane interactions Occur when colloids adsorb or attach to the membrane surface, leading often to irreversible fouling or internal clogging. content: - CmC: direct Colloid–Colloid interactions Become relevant when colloids aggregate or form multilayer de­posits. These are characterized by the cake yield stress, a mechanical extension of osmotic pressure that describes the compressibility of a deposit. These interactions become essential once an adsorption layer or deposit forms on the membrane (see Section 5.1). However, they are not required to describe the initial coupling of interactions that govern mass accumulation and the emergence of counter-osmotic pressure, as observed during concentration polarization. 2.4. Putting it all together: a recipe for complexity In summary, operating a membrane process involves three main ingredients (M, F, C), five essential interactions (FM, CF, CfM, FF, CfC), and two optional direct contact (MC, CC) that enrich the picture when contact occurs. This simple yet comprehensive framework already cap­tures the core of complexity inherent to membrane science. And just like in gastronomy, ingredients alone do not make the recipe: it is the way they bind internally (intra-phase) and interact across phases (inter-phase) that ultimately controls the efficiency of the process (Fig. 2).

content: s the way they bind internally (intra-phase) and interact across phases (inter-phase) that ultimately controls the efficiency of the process (Fig. 2). - The fluid–membrane interaction (FM) determines the frictional resistance and thus the membrane permeability.- The colloid–membrane interaction (CfM) defines the selectivity, through affinity or exclusion mechanisms.- The colloid–fluid interaction (CF) governs the drag force acting on colloids, leading to particle accumulation and additional flow resistance. But this qualitative “taste” of interactions is only the first step. To move toward a predictive understanding, one must describe how these interactions quantitatively balance each other; since their equilibrium determines whether a separation process succeeds or fails. With this framework in mind, we can now transition from the qualitative recipe to the quantitative formulation; where physics, equations, and intuition come together to model and understand transfer through membranes. 3. From interactions to equations It is quite intuitive to imagine how interactions govern transfer through membranes. Yet, to progress from intuition to control and prediction, we need to see how these interactions can be translated into equations. Interactions manifest as forces (Section 3.1), which in turn give rise to transport phenomena (Section 3.2).

These can finally be integrated into field equations (Section 3.3), linking the microscopic picture of interactions to the macroscopic performance of the process. 3.1. Interactions: conversion into force balances To keep the conceptual simplicity of the previous section, we can consider the five fundamental interactions as forces acting between phases. Each force applied to one phase has a reciprocal reaction on the other, according to Newton's third law. These reciprocal actions between phases are illustrated in Fig. 2, where the number of lines directed toward a phase corresponds to the number of forces acting upon it. For instance, in the second row of Table 1, the three forces acting on the colloidal phase are: from the fluid (drag force, CF), from other colloids (colloid–colloid interaction, CfC), and from the membrane (colloid–membrane interaction, CfM). A force balance can thus be written for each phase. By convention, a phase X is influenced by all interactions whose notation contains the letter X. Because every interaction implies a reciprocal exchange, the force applied by one phase is balanced by an opposite reaction on the other.

content: he letter X. Because every interaction implies a reciprocal exchange, the force applied by one phase is balanced by an opposite reaction on the other. This approach, considering each phase as a continuum exchanging forces with the others, is the foundation of the Suspension Balance Model (also known as the mixture model or two-fluid model) [5–8]. In this framework, the mixture velocity (that of the dispersion) is defined as the volume-averaged velocity of the colloids and the fluid molecules: uF+C = ϕuC + (1 -ϕ)uF(1) where ϕ is the colloidal volume fraction. For convenience all equation will be written using volume fraction but they can be converted to molar concentration, cm, or mass concentration, cw, by considering that ϕ = cwρc = Mw cm ρc where ρc and Mw are the density and the molecular weight of colloids. The balance of forces on the fluid and colloidal phases can be combined to obtain the global force balance for the mixture, F + C, which determines the flow of the dispersion. It should be noted that the sum of the force on the membrane (line M) is not equal to zero because the reaction force of the membrane - which does not flow-must be added. A subtlety concerns the colloid–colloid interaction mediated by the fluid, CfC, which involves forces exchanged through the fluid-colloid mixture. Because colloids exhibit Brownian motion, the fluctua­tion–dissipation theorem links their random motion (diffusion) to viscous dissipation in the surrounding fluid.

content: s exhibit Brownian motion, the fluctua­tion–dissipation theorem links their random motion (diffusion) to viscous dissipation in the surrounding fluid. The most famous expression of this link is Einstein's relation, which connects the diffusion coefficient of a Brownian particle to the fluid viscosity, illustrating how the force exerted by the fluid on a colloid (leading to its diffusion) is exactly balanced by the frictional force dissipated in the fluid. It is important to note that this CfC term vanishes in the global balance of the mixture, since it represents internal exchanges between the colloidal and fluid phases. Nevertheless, it plays a key role in describing the relative motion and momentum exchange between these two phases — particularly near the membrane, where slip conditions occur because colloids are repelled or excluded from the membrane surface. These refinements pave the way toward improved models — and, ultimately, toward the design of membranes with enhanced functionality. 5.1. Equation of state for colloid/colloid interactions and phase transitions Colloid–colloid interactions underpin several key phenomena that strongly affect membrane processes. - Repulsion between colloidal particles limits over-concentration near the membrane, since repelling particles are harder to compress.- Attraction can lead to phase transitions and gel formation when cohesive forces overcome the repulsive barrier.

Beyond this critical point, the compressive yield stress governs the layer's compressibility up to close packing [38]. The two contributions, osmotic and compressive, thus allow a continuous description of colloid phases but differ fundamentally. - Osmotic pressure is thermodynamic and reversible: energy brought by the fluid is returned to the flow without external dissipation.- Compressive yield stress is mechanical and dissipative: energy is lost as frictional heat. 5.1.3. They also determine layer reversibility: gel relaxation and redispersion upon pressure release can be captured by models including phase transitions in the EOS [37] (as detailed later in section 6.1). The originality of this modelling model approach is to introduce in the equation all the interactions between the colloids, the membrane and the fluid. Beyond these refinements, one can go further by consid­ering how the different types of pressures (or stresses) — static, osmotic, mechanical, and interfacial — emerge from and couple through the same underlying interactions. For instance, in Brownian sheared dis­persions, shear-induced collisions generate an additional contribution known as the shear induced pressure [43–45]. This contribution can be naturally integrated within the broader concept of colloid pressure, ΠC, in Eq. (20) [46]. Altogether, these results reveal that the colloidal pressure and the associated interaction forces cannot be considered in isolation from the membrane structure.

The transition from bulk-like colloidal behavior to confined interfacial regions introduces new physical regimes where colloid–membrane interactions dominate. The colloid–membrane in­teractions defines a new level of the interaction hierarchy which will be examined in Section 5.2. 5.2. Energy landscape for colloid/membrane interactions Colloid–membrane interactions are central to separation, selectivity, and fouling, yet often underestimated. They govern. - Selectivity, via exclusion mechanisms (steric or electrostatic) [47, 48];- Fouling and critical flux, through the balance of drag and repulsion [3].- Osmotic flow, which arises from colloid-membrane interaction [25]. These interactions appears as an interfacial pressure term, ϕ∇ΠCfM, in both the colloid (Eq. (2)) and momentum (Eq. (3)) balances, reflecting their dual role on mass transport and local pressure [22]. Depending on pore geometry and surface composition, the strength of these interactions varies along the membrane depth. This variation can be represented as an energy landscape: the potential barrier a colloid experiences when approaching or traversing the membrane. Like mountaineers crossing a pass, colloids must overcome or bypass these barriers (a topographic map of elevation), and external driving (the “wind”) can help them through.

Simplified 1D potentials with linear variation of the energy barrier at the pore inlet/outlet enable analytical solutions [49]. Fig. 9 illustrates that interaction stiffness strongly affects transmission and counter-osmotic pressure. - Steep exclusion ramps reduce transmission but increase counterpressure;- Soft ramps lower counter-osmotic pressure by distributing accumu­ lation more smoothly. Asymmetric interactions thus lead to direction-dependent transfer properties as already evidenced by experiments on plasmid DNA transmission [50] in forward and reverse filtration. Extending to 2D or 3D, energy landscapes can capture both pore geometry and surface heterogeneity by simulations (section 6.1.1). Refining these interactions — in both simulations and experiments — is key for innovation in membrane science and in microfluidics/nano­fluidics fields [51–53]. Implementing interfacial pressure fields (in 1D–3D Eulerian models) opens the path to engineered pore architec­tures. Such design strategies could mimic biological pores, where nanoscale mosaics of attractive and repulsive regions create selective and energy-efficient pathways [31,54].

These four pressures (fluid–fluid, FF, colloid–fluid–colloid, CfM, colloid–colloid, CmC, and colloid–fluid–membrane; CfM) do not act in isolation: their spatial gradients combine in Eqs. (2) and (19) to define the driving forces that ultimately govern mass transfer and selectivity across the membrane (Fig. 10). 5.3.1. Colloid driving pressure (CfC + CfM) The gradient of the colloid osmotic pressure, ∇ΠCfC, induces diffu­sion of colloids and drives their accumulation near the membrane. This same term also governs the reversibility of this accumulation during relaxation. In parallel, the gradient of the interfacial pressure, ϕ∇ΠCfM, induces migration of particles away from the membrane due to col­loid–membrane interactions. Together, these two contributions define the relative motion of colloids with respect to the fluid mixture, i.e. the colloid–mixture motion [25]; a difference of velocity that drives the selectivity. uC -uF+C = -Vp m(ϕ)ϕ(∇ΠCfC + ϕ∇ΠCfM

content: 5.3.2. Mixture driving pressure (FF + CfM) The interfacial pressure gradient, ϕ∇ΠCfM, also acts on the solvent phase, giving rise to osmosis at the membrane interface, while the fluid pressure gradient, ∇pFF, drives permeation through the membrane. Their combined effect determines the mixture flow across the interface — the mixture–interface motion — which directly links to the pro­ductivity of the process [25]. uF+C = -kp η (∇pFF + ϕ∇ΠCfM )(24) 5.3.3. Deposit driving pressure (FF + CmC) Within a deposited or gel layer, the fluid pressure gradient, ∇pFF, still drives solvent filtration, while the compressive yield stress gradient, ∇ΠCmC, characterizes the resistance of the deposit to compaction. The interplay between these two pressures governs the filtration dynamics and mechanical consolidation of the deposit, i.e. the mixture–deposit motion. uF+C = -kp η (∇pFF + ∇ΠCmC)(25) Overall, these three coupled driving forces control both the pro­ductivity (mixture velocity with the effect of counter osmotic pressure and fouling layers) and the selectivity (relative motion of colloids vs. mixture) in the general case where concentration polarization and de­posit formation coexist. However, these three driving forces act simultaneously and are often tightly coupled in membrane processes. They interact and redistribute in space, especially once the membrane interface is considered beyond a single dimension.

How interactions complexity can shape separation? The refinement introduced in the previous section provides the tools to approach the inherent complexity of mass transfer through mem­branes. To illustrate this, three examples are developed here, each addressing a distinct question on how interactions govern separation mechanisms, and each relying on specific numerical recipes [55] adapted to the constraints of the problem. - How colloid-colloid phase transition influence the relaxation of an accumulated phase? This question is examined through the refine­ment of the colloid equation of state (a refined CfC, section 5.1) using open source CFD simulations to highlight the role of the dis­persion–gel transition in fouling and lumen plugging in hollow fibers.- How do pore-scale interactions lead to the establishment of os­ motic fluxes? This point is explored using a 2D energy landscape describing colloid–membrane interactions (a refined CfM, section 5.2), in order to characterize the local dynamics that generate os­motic flow.

6.1. How colloid-colloid phase transition can lead to membrane plugging? The way the dispersion–gel transition operates at the membrane interface, and its consequences on fouling reversibility, are still under investigation [56]. The main difficulty lies in the complexity of the phase transition and in its proper integration into modeling approaches [57]. During this transition, attractive colloidal forces dominate and can produce specific diffusion behaviors [37], possibly explaining the reversible nature of the process. The relaxation of gel layers was investigated in a hollow-fiber configuration using 2D axi-symetrical model in CFD simulations (OpenFOAM). The phase transition physics was incorporated into the diffusion term [58] through a concentration-dependent diffusion coef­ficient, following the equation of state (Fig. 5) via a generalized Sto­kes–Einstein relation (Eq. (9)). Simulations show in Fig. 11 that gel layer relaxation results from a combination of slow diffusive removal and rapid expansion of the compressed gel once the applied pressure is released [35]. Under certain conditions, this expansion can block the lumen of hollow fibers, leading to severe membrane plugging.

content: 6.2. How colloid-membrane interactions establish osmotic fluxes? Osmotic fluxes are generally described macroscopically as solvent flows induced by concentration difference. However, the local dynamics that drive their development within membrane pores remain poorly understood [26,60]. To elucidate this, it is essential to investigate how colloid–membrane interactions (CfM) combine with hydrodynamic flow to trigger local diffusio-osmotic effects, which collectively give rise to macroscopic osmotic fluxes (yellow pathway in Fig. 3). Simulations based on a one-dimensional representation of CfM in­teractions (Section 5.2) can describe how these mechanisms govern osmotic flux dynamics when a concentration gradient approaches the membrane [25]. Replacing the 1D colloid–membrane energy landscape by 2D colloid–wall interaction map [23] allows a detailed visualization of how osmotic fluxes originate at the pore scale and how their distri­bution evolves under the influence of local mass accumulation patterns near the pore entrance (Fig. 10). Results from simulations [23,61] (python code is given in SI 2) reveal how accumulation patterns evolve with the filtration P´eclet number and identify a critical Pe marking the transition between plug formation and wall accumulation (Fig. 12a). content: 6.3. How colloids clogs change according colloid-colloid and colloidmembrane interactions? Recent advances in microfluidics have provided valuable insights into colloidal accumulation and fouling dynamics in pore networks mimicking membranes [63–67]. These operando observations reveal the complexity of local clogging events and offer a benchmark to assess whether simulation ingredients can reproduce such behavior. A realistic description of pore clogging must account for both col­loid–colloid (CfC) and colloid–membrane (CfM) interactions. To pre­serve particle discreteness -rather than relying on averaged concentration fieldsand to properly address the complexity of ternary membrane–colloid–fluid interactions, 2D or 3D Lagrangian simulations are required [20,68]. In this framework, the fluid flow is solved from the momentum equation (Eq. (19)), while mass transport is replaced by tracking individual colloids subjected to CfC and CfM forces. This can be achieved, for instance, through iterative coupling between flow field computation (e.g., via the Force Coupling Method, FCM) and the calculation of particle trajectories [20,69]. Different interaction strengths produce distinct clogging morphol­ogies (Fig. 13). When both colloid–colloid and colloid–wall repulsions are strong, no deposit forms; when they decrease, deposits emerge above a critical flux (from top right to bottom left in Fig. 13).

Conclusions The physical recipe presented here reformulates membrane transport as the result of a structured interplay between interactions. By dis­tinguishing the roles of fluid, colloid, and membrane phases — and explicitly accounting for their mutual interactions — we reveal a mini­mal yet complete set of couplings that explain key membrane phe­nomena. The approach offers a simple and pedagogical entry point into the membrane science field, starting from the intuition of the interaction and delaying as much as possible the painful conversion into equations. By explicitly representing these interactions in the governing equa­tions, a minimal yet comprehensive set of couplings is identified, capable of describing permeation, polarization, fouling, selectivity, and osmotic phenomena within a unified framework.

content: et of couplings is identified, capable of describing permeation, polarization, fouling, selectivity, and osmotic phenomena within a unified framework. This unified, interaction-based formulation also bridges several classical quantities of membrane science (pressure, flux, selectivity) into a single physically consistent language from diluted to solid phases. This interaction-based perspective highlights that complexity in membrane systems arises from the subtle hierarchy of interactions acting between phases. Understanding, quantifying these interactions offers a route to control and predict membrane transfer. Examples of simulations highlight how the combination of interaction-based modeling and numerical tools can reproduce experimentally observed features and guide practical design strategies for fouling mitigation and performance optimization. By designing and manipulating the in­teractions in 3D, one can envision tailored pores that combine the ad­vantages of membranes and chromatographic systems, mimicking biological architectures.
